title: Development and Validation of a Deep-Learning Model to Screen for Hyperkalemia From the Electrocardiogram

items.abstract: <jats:p>Background: Hyperkalemia is a life‑threatening complication in chronic kidney disease (CKD) that affects clinical outcomes and limits use of guideline‑directed therapies. Existing prediction tools lack accuracy and generalizability across diverse healthcare settings. We developed and externally validated a machine learning model for hyperkalemia prediction using multinational data.&nbsp;<br><br>Methods: In this retrospective cohort study, we included 69,242 adults with CKD stages 3‑5 from 12 countries across five continents (2015‑2023). The derivation cohort comprised 46,431 patients from seven countries; external validation used 22,811 patients from five additional countries. A gradient boosting machine incorporating clinical and laboratory variables was developed. Model performance was assessed by AUC, calibration metrics, and decision curve analysis. The optimal intervention threshold was identified through cutoff analysis. The primary composite outcome was defined as: (1) any of all‑cause death, resuscitated critical event, or ≥3 hospitalizations within 12 months; AND (2) a sustained eGFR decline &gt;40% from baseline.&nbsp;<br><br>Findings: The model showed robust discrimination in external validation (AUC 0.86, 95% CI 0.84‑0.88), significantly better than existing clinical rules (p&lt;0.001). Calibration was excellent (intercept ‑0.03, slope 1.02). Decision curve analysis confirmed net benefit across probability thresholds 5‑25%. The optimal intervention threshold of 15% predicted probability balanced sensitivity (78%) and specificity (82%). Model performance remained consistent across CKD stages, age groups, and geographic regions (AUC range 0.83‑0.88).&nbsp;<br><br>Interpretation: This multinational validation establishes a generalizable machine learning approach for hyperkalemia prediction in CKD populations. The model's consistent performance across diverse settings and identified intervention threshold provide a practical tool for risk stratification and clinical decision support in global healthcare systems.</jats:p>
title: Development and Multinational Validation of a Machine Learning Model for Predicting Hyperkalemia in Chronic Kidney Disease

container-title: Journal of the American Society of Nephrology
subtitle: FR-PO543
title: Development and Validation of Deep Learning Algorithm for Detecting Hyperkalemia Based on Electrocardiogram

items.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
          <jats:sec>
            <jats:title>Background:</jats:title>
            <jats:p>A deep learning model (DLM) that enables non-invasive hypokalemia screening from an electrocardiogram (ECG) may improve the detection of this life-threatening condition. This study aimed to develop and evaluate the performance of a DLM for the detection of hypokalemia from the ECGs of emergency patients.</jats:p>
          </jats:sec>
          <jats:sec>
            <jats:title>Methods:</jats:title>
            <jats:p>We used a total of 9908 ECG data from emergency patients who were admitted at the Second Affiliated Hospital of Nanchang University, Jiangxi, China, from September 2017 to October 2020. The DLM was trained using 12 ECG leads (lead I, II, III, aVR, aVL, aVF, and V<jats:sub>1</jats:sub>–<jats:sub>6</jats:sub>) to detect patients with serum potassium concentrations <3.5 mmol/L and was validated using retrospective data from the Jiangling branch of the Second Affiliated Hospital of Nanchang University. The blood draw was completed within 10 min before and after the ECG examination, and there was no new or ongoing infusion during this period.</jats:p>
          </jats:sec>
          <jats:sec>
            <jats:title>Results:</jats:title>
            <jats:p>We used 6904 ECGs and 1726 ECGs as development and internal validation data sets, respectively. In addition, 1278 ECGs from the Jiangling branch of the Second Affiliated Hospital of Nanchang University were used as external validation data sets. Using 12 ECG leads (leads I, II, III, aVR, aVL, aVF, and V<jats:sub>1</jats:sub>–<jats:sub>6</jats:sub>), the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of the DLM was 0.80 (95% confidence interval [CI]: 0.77–0.82) for the internal validation data set. Using an optimal operating point yielded a sensitivity of 71.4% and a specificity of 77.1%. Using the same 12 ECG leads, the external validation data set resulted in an AUC for the DLM of 0.77 (95% CI: 0.75–0.79). Using an optimal operating point yielded a sensitivity of 70.0% and a specificity of 69.1%.</jats:p>
          </jats:sec>
          <jats:sec>
            <jats:title>Conclusions:</jats:title>
            <jats:p>In this study, using 12 ECG leads, a DLM detected hypokalemia in emergency patients with an AUC of 0.77 to 0.80. Artificial intelligence could be used to analyze an ECG to quickly screen for hypokalemia.</jats:p>
          </jats:sec>
